title: endtry
text: endtry Defines the end of a try...endtry block used to handle exceptions. Explain this code try <ProcScript that may throw an exception> {catch} {ErrorNumber1 {, ErrorNumberN}} <Procscript that handles the error >} {rethrow} {finally} <ProcScript that will always execute, regardless of whether an error occurred or not> endtry Copy Use Allowed in all component types. Description For each try declaration, it is possible to have multiple catch statements to test for specific errors, as well as a rethrow statement, and/or a finally block. The endtry command must come at the end. For more information, see try . Related Links Exception Handling Exceptions are abnormal runtime events that may occur after executing a software instruction. Uncaught exceptions cause code execution to stop and the application to exit with a description of the error and where it occurred. try A try block specifies a ProcScript instruction, or set of instructions, that could raise an exception. catch In a try...endtry block, introduces the code to catch and handle exceptions. finally Declares ProcScript inside a try-endtry block that must be executed, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not. rethrow Rethrows an exception that was caught in a catch block.
